question_text: Prove that {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin x}+\sqrt{1-\sin x}}{\sqrt{1+\sin x}-\sqrt{1-\sin x}}\right\}=\frac{x}{2}{/tex} , {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_html: Prove that {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin x}+\sqrt{1-\sin x}}{\sqrt{1+\sin x}-\sqrt{1-\sin x}}\right\}=\frac{x}{2}{/tex} , {tex}0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\text { L.H.S. }=\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin x}+\sqrt{1-\sin x}}{\sqrt{1+\sin x}-\sqrt{1-\sin x}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}+\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}{\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}-\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|+\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|}{\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|-\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { since } 0<\frac{x}{2}<\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \cos \frac{x}{2}>\sin \frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}+\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}-\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2 \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2}}\right\}=\cot ^{-1}\left\{\cot \frac{x}{2}\right\}=\frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { since } 0<\frac{x}{2}<\frac{\pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text { L.H.S. }=\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin x}+\sqrt{1-\sin x}}{\sqrt{1+\sin x}-\sqrt{1-\sin x}}\right\}{/tex}
= {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}+\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}{\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}-\sqrt{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}}\right\}{/tex}  
= {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|+\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|}{\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|-\left|\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right|}\right\}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\text { since } 0<\frac{x}{2}<\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \cos \frac{x}{2}>\sin \frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}+\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}-\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right\}{/tex}
= {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2 \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2}}\right\}=\cot ^{-1}\left\{\cot \frac{x}{2}\right\}=\frac{x}{2}{/tex}  
{tex}\left[\text { since } 0<\frac{x}{2}<\frac{\pi}{4}\right]{/tex}

question_text: Solve the equation for x if {tex}\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} 2 x=\frac{\pi}{3}, x>0{/tex}
question_html: Solve the equation for x if {tex}\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} 2 x=\frac{\pi}{3}, x>0{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} 2 x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin ^{-1} 2 x=\frac{\pi}{3}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 x=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-\sin ^{-1} x\right){/tex} 1 = {tex}\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)-\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)}-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex} {tex}4 x-\sqrt{3} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-x, 5 x-\sqrt{3} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}25 x^{2}=3\left(1-x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}28 x^{2}=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}=\frac{3}{28}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\pm \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{3}{7}}{/tex} Hence {tex}x=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{3}{7}}(\text { as } x>0 \text { given }){/tex} Thus {tex}x=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{3}{7}}{/tex} is the solution of given equation.
solution_html:
{tex}\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} 2 x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sin ^{-1} 2 x=\frac{\pi}{3}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 x=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-\sin ^{-1} x\right){/tex}  1
= {tex}\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)-\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}{tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)}-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex}
{tex}4 x-\sqrt{3} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-x, 5 x-\sqrt{3} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex}  
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}25 x^{2}=3\left(1-x^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}28 x^{2}=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}=\frac{3}{28}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\pm \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{3}{7}}{/tex}  
Hence {tex}x=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{3}{7}}(\text { as } x>0 \text { given }){/tex}  
Thus {tex}x=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{3}{7}}{/tex} is the solution of given equation.

question_text: Using properties of determinants, prove that {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} b+c & c+a & a+b \\ q+r & r+p & p+q \\ y+z & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|=2\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ p & q & r \\ x & y & z \end{array}\right|{/tex}
question_html:
Using properties of determinants, prove that
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} b+c & c+a & a+b \\ q+r & r+p & p+q \\ y+z & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|=2\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ p & q & r \\ x & y & z \end{array}\right|{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\text { Let } \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} b+c & c+a & a+b \\ q+r & r+p & p+q \\ y+z & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|{/tex} Using C 1 → C 1 + C 2 + C 3 , we get {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 2(a+b+c) & c+a & a+b \\ 2(p+q+r) & r+p & p+q \\ 2(x+y+z) & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|{/tex} ...(1) = {tex}2\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a+b+c & c+a & a+b \\ p+q+r & r+p & p+q \\ x+y+z & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|{/tex} Using C 2 → C 2 –C 1 and C 3 → C 3 –C 1 , we get {tex}\Delta=2\left|\begin{array}{lll} a+b+c & -b & -c \\ p+q+r & -q & -r \\ x+y+z & -y & -z \end{array}\right|{/tex} Using C 1 → C 1 + C 2 + C 3 and taking (– 1) common from both C 2 and C 3 {tex}\Delta=2\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ p & q & r \\ x & y & z \end{array}\right|{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text { Let } \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} b+c & c+a & a+b \\ q+r & r+p & p+q \\ y+z & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Using C1 → C1 + C2 + C3 , we get
{tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 2(a+b+c) & c+a & a+b \\ 2(p+q+r) & r+p & p+q \\ 2(x+y+z) & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|{/tex}   ...(1)
= {tex}2\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a+b+c & c+a & a+b \\ p+q+r & r+p & p+q \\ x+y+z & z+x & x+y \end{array}\right|{/tex}
Using C2 → C2 –C1 and C3 → C3 –C1 , we get
{tex}\Delta=2\left|\begin{array}{lll} a+b+c & -b & -c \\ p+q+r & -q & -r \\ x+y+z & -y & -z \end{array}\right|{/tex}  
Using C1 → C1 + C2 + C3 and taking (– 1) common from both C2 and C3
{tex}\Delta=2\left|\begin{array}{lll} a & b & c \\ p & q & r \\ x & y & z \end{array}\right|{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f given by f(x) = |x + 1| + |x + 2| at x = -1 and x = -2.
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f given by f(x) = |x + 1| + |x + 2| at x = -1 and x = -2.
solution_text: Case 1 when {tex}x<-2{/tex} f (x) = |x + 1| + |x + 2| = – (x + 1) – (x + 2) = –2x –3 Case 2 When – 2 {tex}\le{/tex} x < –1 f (x) = –x – 1 + x + 2 = 1 Case 3 When {tex}\ge{/tex} –1 f (x) = x + 1 + x + 2 = 2x + 3 Thus {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} -2 x-3 & \text { when } & x<-2 \\ 1 & \text { when } & -2 \leq x<-1 \\ 2 x+3 & \text { when } & x \geq-1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now, L.H.S at {tex}x=-2, \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{-}}(-2 x-3)=4-3=1{/tex} R.H.S at {tex}x=-2, \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} 1=1{/tex} Also {tex}f(-2)=|-2+1|+|-2+2|=|-1|+|0|=1{/tex} Thus, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-2} f(x)=f(-2)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The function f is continuous at {tex}x=-2{/tex} Now, L.H.S at {tex}x=-1, \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} 1=1{/tex} R.H.S at {tex}x=-1, \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}}(2 x+3)=1{/tex} {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Also {tex}f(-1)=|-1+1|+|-1+2|=1{/tex} Thus, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}}=f(-1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The function is continuous at x = -1 Hence, the given function is continuous at both the points x = -1 and x = -2.
solution_html:
Case 1 when {tex}x<-2{/tex}
f (x) = |x + 1| + |x + 2| = – (x + 1) – (x + 2) = –2x –3
Case 2 When – 2 {tex}\le{/tex} x < –1
f (x) = –x – 1 + x + 2 = 1
Case 3 When {tex}\ge{/tex} –1
f (x) = x + 1 + x + 2 = 2x + 3
Thus
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} -2 x-3 & \text { when } & x<-2 \\ 1 & \text { when } & -2 \leq x<-1 \\ 2 x+3 & \text { when } & x \geq-1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Now, L.H.S at {tex}x=-2, \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{-}}(-2 x-3)=4-3=1{/tex}
R.H.S at {tex}x=-2, \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} 1=1{/tex}
Also {tex}f(-2)=|-2+1|+|-2+2|=|-1|+|0|=1{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-2} f(x)=f(-2)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} f(x){/tex}   {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The function f is continuous at {tex}x=-2{/tex}
Now, L.H.S at {tex}x=-1, \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} 1=1{/tex}
R.H.S at {tex}x=-1, \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} f(x){/tex}
= {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}}(2 x+3)=1{/tex}  {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Also {tex}f(-1)=|-1+1|+|-1+2|=1{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}}=f(-1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The function is continuous at x = -1
Hence, the given function is continuous at both the points x = -1 and x = -2.

question_text: If x = {tex}\theta{/tex} – cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2sin {tex}\theta{/tex} – sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
question_html: If x = {tex}\theta{/tex} – cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2sin{tex}\theta{/tex} – sin2{tex}\theta{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
solution_text: x = 2cos {tex}\theta{/tex} – cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} – sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} So {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{\theta}}=\frac{\cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta}=\frac{-2 \sin \frac{3 \theta}{2} \sin \left(\frac{-\theta}{2}\right)}{2 \cos \frac{3 \theta}{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}}=\tan \frac{3 \theta}{2}{/tex} {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{3}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2(\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta)}=\frac{3}{4} \sec ^{2} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2 \cos \frac{3 \theta}{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{8} \sec ^{3} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \csc \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} Thus {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \text { is } \frac{3}{8} \sec ^{3} \frac{3 \pi}{4} \csc \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
x = 2cos{tex}\theta{/tex}– cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin{tex}\theta{/tex} – sin2{tex}\theta{/tex}
So {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{\theta}}=\frac{\cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta}=\frac{-2 \sin \frac{3 \theta}{2} \sin \left(\frac{-\theta}{2}\right)}{2 \cos \frac{3 \theta}{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}}=\tan \frac{3 \theta}{2}{/tex}  {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{3}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2(\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta)}=\frac{3}{4} \sec ^{2} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2 \cos \frac{3 \theta}{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{8} \sec ^{3} \frac{3 \theta}{2} \csc \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} 
Thus {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \text { is } \frac{3}{8} \sec ^{3} \frac{3 \pi}{4} \csc \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{-3}{2}{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0, \text { prove that } \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}, {/tex} where -1 < x < 1
question_html: If {tex}x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0, \text { prove that } \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}, {/tex} where -1 < x < 1
solution_text: ATQ, we can write , {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x}{/tex} Squaring both sides, we get {tex}x^{2}(1+y)=y^{2}(1+x){/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x+y)(x-y)=-y x(x-y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x+y=-x y, \text { i.e., } y=\frac{-x}{1+x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left\{\frac{(1+x) \cdot 1-x(0+1)}{(1+x)^{2}}\right\}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
ATQ, we can write , 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x}{/tex}
Squaring both sides, we get
{tex}x^{2}(1+y)=y^{2}(1+x){/tex}  1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x+y)(x-y)=-y x(x-y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x+y=-x y, \text { i.e., } y=\frac{-x}{1+x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left\{\frac{(1+x) \cdot 1-x(0+1)}{(1+x)^{2}}\right\}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: A cone is 10cm in diameter and 10cm deep. Water is poured into it at the rate of 4 cubic cm per minute. At what rate is the water level rising at the instant when the depth is 6cm?
question_html: A cone is 10cm in diameter and 10cm deep. Water is poured into it at the rate of 4 cubic cm per minute. At what rate is the water level rising at the instant when the depth is 6cm?
solution_text: Let OAB be a cone and let LM be the level of water at any time t. Let ON = h and MN = r Given AB = 10 cm, OC = 10 cm and {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \mathrm{cm}^{3}{/tex} minute, where V denotes the volume of cone OLM. Note that {tex}\triangle{/tex} ONM {tex}\sim{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} OCB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{M N}{C B}=\frac{O N}{O C} \text { or } \frac{r}{5}=\frac{h}{10} \Rightarrow r=\frac{h}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} .......(i) Substituting {tex}r=\frac{h}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get {tex}V=\frac{1}{12} \pi h^{3}{/tex} {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Differentiating w.r.t.t {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d t}=\frac{3 \pi h^{2}}{12} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{4}{\pi h^{2}} \frac{d v}{d t}{/tex} Therefore, when h = 6 cm, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{4}{9 \pi}{/tex} cm/minute
solution_html:
Let OAB be a cone and let LM be the level of water at any time t.
Let ON = h and MN = r
Given AB = 10 cm, OC = 10 cm and {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=4 \mathrm{cm}^{3}{/tex} minute, where V denotes the volume of cone OLM.
Note that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ONM {tex}\sim{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex} OCB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{M N}{C B}=\frac{O N}{O C} \text { or } \frac{r}{5}=\frac{h}{10} \Rightarrow r=\frac{h}{2}{/tex}  
Now, {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex}  .......(i)
Substituting {tex}r=\frac{h}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get 
{tex}V=\frac{1}{12} \pi h^{3}{/tex}   {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Differentiating w.r.t.t
{tex}\frac{d \mathrm{V}}{d t}=\frac{3 \pi h^{2}}{12} \frac{d h}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{4}{\pi h^{2}} \frac{d v}{d t}{/tex}
Therefore, when h = 6 cm, {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{4}{9 \pi}{/tex} cm/minute

question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f is given by {tex}f(x)=x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}, x \neq 0 \text { is }{/tex} increasing decreasing
question_html:
Find the intervals in which the function f is given by {tex}f(x)=x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}, x \neq 0 \text { is }{/tex} 

  1. increasing
  2. decreasing
solution_text: {tex}f(x)=x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{3}-\frac{3}{x^{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3\left(x^{6}-1\right)}{x^{4}}=\frac{3\left(x^{2}-1\right)\left(x^{4}+x^{2}+1\right)}{x^{4}}{/tex} {tex}\text { As } x^{4}+x^{2}+1>0{/tex} and {tex}x^{4}>0{/tex} , therefore, for f to be increasing, we have {tex}x^{2}-1>0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \in(-\infty,-1) \cup(1, \infty){/tex} Thus f is increasing in {tex}(-\infty,-1) \cup(1, \infty){/tex} For f to be decreasing {tex}f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}-1<0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x-1)(x+1)<0 \Rightarrow x \in(-1,0) \cup(0,1){/tex} {tex}[x \neq 0 \text { as } f \text { is not defined at } x=0]{/tex} Thus f (x) is decreasing in {tex}(-1,0) \cup(0,1){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{3}-\frac{3}{x^{4}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3\left(x^{6}-1\right)}{x^{4}}=\frac{3\left(x^{2}-1\right)\left(x^{4}+x^{2}+1\right)}{x^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { As } x^{4}+x^{2}+1>0{/tex} and {tex}x^{4}>0{/tex}, therefore, for f to be increasing, we have
{tex}x^{2}-1>0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \in(-\infty,-1) \cup(1, \infty){/tex}
Thus f is increasing in {tex}(-\infty,-1) \cup(1, \infty){/tex}
For f to be decreasing {tex}f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}-1<0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x-1)(x+1)<0 \Rightarrow x \in(-1,0) \cup(0,1){/tex} {tex}[x \neq 0 \text { as } f \text { is not defined at } x=0]{/tex}  
Thus f (x) is decreasing in {tex}(-1,0) \cup(0,1){/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}}=\frac{A}{x+3}+\frac{B}{x+1}+\frac{C}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} Then 3x – 2 = A (x + 1) 2 + B (x + 1) (x + 3) + C (x + 3) comparing the coefficient of x 2 , x and constant, we get A + B = 0, 2A + 4B + C = 3 and A + 3B + 3C = –2 Solving these equations, we get {tex}A=\frac{-11}{4}, B=\frac{11}{4} \text { and } C=\frac{-5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}}=\frac{-11}{4(x+3)}+\frac{11}{4(x+1)}-\frac{5}{2(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} Hence {tex}\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}} d x=\frac{-11}{4} \int_{x+3}^{1} d x+\frac{11}{4} \frac{1}{x+1} d x-\frac{5}{2} \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-11}{4} \log |x+3|+\frac{11}{4} \log |x+1|+\frac{5}{2(x+1)}+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}}=\frac{A}{x+3}+\frac{B}{x+1}+\frac{C}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex}
Then 3x – 2 = A (x + 1)2 + B (x + 1) (x + 3) + C (x + 3)
comparing the coefficient of x2 , x and constant, we get
A + B = 0, 2A + 4B + C = 3 and A + 3B + 3C = –2
Solving these equations, we get
{tex}A=\frac{-11}{4}, B=\frac{11}{4} \text { and } C=\frac{-5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}}=\frac{-11}{4(x+3)}+\frac{11}{4(x+1)}-\frac{5}{2(x+1)^{2}}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+3)(x+1)^{2}} d x=\frac{-11}{4} \int_{x+3}^{1} d x+\frac{11}{4} \frac{1}{x+1} d x-\frac{5}{2} \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-11}{4} \log |x+3|+\frac{11}{4} \log |x+1|+\frac{5}{2(x+1)}+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int\left(\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int\left(\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\int\left(\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \log (\log x) d x+\int \frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}} d x{/tex} Integrating log (logx) by parts, we get {tex}\int \log (\log x) d x=x \log (\log x)-\frac{f^{x}}{(\log x)} \times \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}x \log (\log x)-\int \frac{1}{\log x} d x{/tex} {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}x \log (\log x)-\left[\frac{x}{\log x}-\int x\left(\frac{-1}{(\log x)^{2}}\right) \times \frac{1}{x} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}x \log (\log x)-\frac{x}{\log x}-\int \frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}} d x{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int\left(\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}}\right) d x=x \log (\log x)-\frac{x}{\log x}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int\left(\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \log (\log x) d x+\int \frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}} d x{/tex}
Integrating log (logx) by parts, we get
{tex}\int \log (\log x) d x=x \log (\log x)-\frac{f^{x}}{(\log x)} \times \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}x \log (\log x)-\int \frac{1}{\log x} d x{/tex}    {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}x \log (\log x)-\left[\frac{x}{\log x}-\int x\left(\frac{-1}{(\log x)^{2}}\right) \times \frac{1}{x} d x\right]{/tex}
= {tex}x \log (\log x)-\frac{x}{\log x}-\int \frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}} d x{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\int\left(\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{(\log x)^{2}}\right) d x=x \log (\log x)-\frac{x}{\log x}+c{/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
solution_text: Let I = {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x\left[\operatorname{since}\left[(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]\right.{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put cos x = t for {tex}x=\pi \Rightarrow t=-1, x=0 \Rightarrow t=+1{/tex} and {tex}-\sin x d x=d t{/tex} Therefore {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{-d t}{1+t^{2}}=\pi \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^{1}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(+1)-\tan ^{-1}(-1)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\pi\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x\left[\operatorname{since}\left[(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]\right.{/tex}
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
Put cos x = t for {tex}x=\pi \Rightarrow t=-1, x=0 \Rightarrow t=+1{/tex} and {tex}-\sin x d x=d t{/tex}
Therefore {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{-d t}{1+t^{2}}=\pi \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^{1}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(+1)-\tan ^{-1}(-1)\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\pi\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}

question_text: Find the differential equation of all the circles which pass through the origin and whose centres lie on x-axis.
question_html: Find the differential equation of all the circles which pass through the origin and whose centres lie on x-axis.
solution_text: The equation of circles which pass through the origin and whose centre lies on x-axis is {tex}(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}{/tex} ......(i) Differentiating w.r.t.x, we get {tex}2(x-a)+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x+y \frac{d y}{d x}=a{/tex} {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Substituting the value of a in (i), we get {tex}\left(y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+y^{2}=\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
The equation of circles which pass through the origin and whose centre lies on x-axis is
{tex}(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}{/tex} ......(i) 
Differentiating w.r.t.x, we get
{tex}2(x-a)+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x+y \frac{d y}{d x}=a{/tex}   {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Substituting the value of a in (i), we get
{tex}\left(y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+y^{2}=\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}

question_text: Solve the differential equation x 2 ydx - (x 3 + y 3 ) dy = 0
question_html: Solve the differential equation x2ydx - (x3 + y3) dy = 0
solution_text: The given differential equation is {tex}x^{2} y d x-\left(x^{3}+y^{3}\right) d y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2} y}{x^{3}+y^{3}}{/tex} .....(i) Put {tex}y=v x \text { so that } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{3}}{x^{3}+v^{3} x^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v}{1+v^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-v^{4}}{1+v^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1+v^{3}}{v^{4}} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{v^{4}} d v+\int \frac{1}{v} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{3 v^{3}}+\log |v|=-\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-x^{3}}{3 y^{3}}+\log |y|=c{/tex} , is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is
{tex}x^{2} y d x-\left(x^{3}+y^{3}\right) d y=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2} y}{x^{3}+y^{3}}{/tex}   .....(i)
Put {tex}y=v x \text { so that } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{3}}{x^{3}+v^{3} x^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v}{1+v^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-v^{4}}{1+v^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1+v^{3}}{v^{4}} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{v^{4}} d v+\int \frac{1}{v} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{3 v^{3}}+\log |v|=-\log |x|+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-x^{3}}{3 y^{3}}+\log |y|=c{/tex} ,  is the required solution.

question_text: If {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}, \vec{a} \neq \overrightarrow{0}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \neq \vec{c}{/tex} , show that {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}+\lambda \vec{a}{/tex} for some scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}, \vec{a} \neq \overrightarrow{0}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \neq \vec{c}{/tex}, show that {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}+\lambda \vec{a}{/tex} for some scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
solution_text: We have {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { or } \vec{b}-\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { or } \vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c})[\text { since } \vec{a} \neq \overrightarrow{0} \& \vec{b} \neq \vec{c}]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}-\vec{c}=\lambda \vec{a}, \text { for some scalar } \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}+\lambda \vec{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times(\vec{b}-\vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { or } \vec{b}-\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0} \text { or } \vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \|(\vec{b}-\vec{c})[\text { since } \vec{a} \neq \overrightarrow{0} \& \vec{b} \neq \vec{c}]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}-\vec{c}=\lambda \vec{a}, \text { for some scalar } \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{c}+\lambda \vec{a}{/tex}

question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(\lambda-1) \hat{i}+(\lambda+1) \hat{j}-(1+\lambda) \hat{k} \text { and } \vec{r}=(1-\vec{\mu}) \hat{i}+(2 \vec{\mu}-1) \hat{j}+(\vec{\mu}+2) \hat{k}{/tex}
question_html: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(\lambda-1) \hat{i}+(\lambda+1) \hat{j}-(1+\lambda) \hat{k} \text { and } \vec{r}=(1-\vec{\mu}) \hat{i}+(2 \vec{\mu}-1) \hat{j}+(\vec{\mu}+2) \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_text: We know that the shorest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{r}=\vec{c}+\mu \vec{d}{/tex} is given by {tex}D=| \frac{(\vec{c}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{d})}{|\vec{b} \times \vec{d}|}{/tex} Now given equations can be written as {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \text { and } r=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\boldsymbol{\mu}(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Therefore {tex}\vec{c}-\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{d}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \vec{i} & \vec{j} & \vec{k} \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=3 \vec{i}-0 . \vec{j}+3 \vec{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b} \times \vec{d}|=\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Hence {tex}\mathrm{D}=\left|\frac{(\vec{c}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{d})}{|\vec{b} \times \vec{d}|}\right|=\left|\frac{6-0+9}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right|=\frac{15}{3 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that the shorest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b} \text { and } \vec{r}=\vec{c}+\mu \vec{d}{/tex} is given by 
{tex}D=| \frac{(\vec{c}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{d})}{|\vec{b} \times \vec{d}|}{/tex} 
Now given equations can be written as
{tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \text { and } r=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\boldsymbol{\mu}(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
Therefore {tex}\vec{c}-\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} 
and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{d}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \vec{i} & \vec{j} & \vec{k} \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|=3 \vec{i}-0 . \vec{j}+3 \vec{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b} \times \vec{d}|=\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} 
Hence {tex}\mathrm{D}=\left|\frac{(\vec{c}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{d})}{|\vec{b} \times \vec{d}|}\right|=\left|\frac{6-0+9}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right|=\frac{15}{3 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex}

question_text: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn and found to be hearts. Find the probability of the missing card to be a heart.
question_html: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn and found to be hearts. Find the probability of the missing card to be a heart.
solution_text: Let E 1 , E 2 , E 3 , E 4 and A be the events defined as follows : E 1 = the missing card is a heart card, E 2 = the missing card is a spade card, E 3 = the missing card is a club card, E 4 = the missing card is a diamond card A = Drawing two heart cards from the remaining cards. Then {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{4}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P (A/E 1 ) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one heart card is missing = {tex}\frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{31} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} P (A/E 2 ) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one spade card is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} Similarly, we have P {tex}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)=\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A / E_{t}\right)=\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{s_{1} C_{2}}{/tex} By Baye’s thereon, we have the required Probability = P {tex}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} / \mathrm{A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{4}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{4}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4} \frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{12 \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{66}{66+78+78+78}=\frac{11}{50}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1, E2 , E3 , E4 and A be the events defined as follows :
E1 = the missing card is a heart card,
E2 = the missing card is a spade card,
E3 = the missing card is a club card,
E4 = the missing card is a diamond card
A = Drawing two heart cards from the remaining cards.
Then {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}, P\left(E_{4}\right)=\frac{13}{52}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
P (A/E1) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one heart card is missing = {tex}\frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{31} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}
P (A/E2) = Probability of drawing two heart cards given that one spade card is missing = {tex}\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex}
Similarly, we have P {tex}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)=\frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A / E_{t}\right)=\frac{^{13} C_{2}}{s_{1} C_{2}}{/tex}
By Baye’s thereon, we have the
required Probability = P {tex}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} / \mathrm{A}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{4}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{E}_{4}\right)}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4} \frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{51} \mathrm{C}_{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1^{12} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{12 \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}+^{13} \mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{66}{66+78+78+78}=\frac{11}{50}{/tex}

question_text: Let the two matrices A and B be given by {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Verify that AB = BA = 6I, where I is the unit matrix of order 3 and hence solve the system of equations {tex}x-y=3,2 x+3 y+4 z=17 \text { and } y+2 z=7{/tex}
question_html:
Let the two matrices A and B be given by
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right] \text { and } B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Verify that AB = BA = 6I, where I is the unit matrix of order 3 and hence solve the system of equations
{tex}x-y=3,2 x+3 y+4 z=17 \text { and } y+2 z=7{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right]=6\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=6I{/tex} Similarly BA = 6I, Hence AB = 6I = BA As {tex}A B=6 I, A^{-1}(A B)=6 A^{-1} I{/tex} . This gives {tex}\mathrm{IB}=6 \mathrm{A}^{-1}, \text { i.e., } \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} The given system of equations can be written as AX = C, where {tex}\mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right], \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ 17 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} The solution of the given system AX = C is given by X = A -1 C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ 17 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 6+34-28 \\ -12+34-28 \\ 6-17+34 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ -1 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence x = 2, y = 1 and z = 4
solution_html:
We have
{tex}A B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right]=6\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=6I{/tex}
Similarly BA = 6I, Hence AB = 6I = BA
As {tex}A B=6 I, A^{-1}(A B)=6 A^{-1} I{/tex} . This gives
{tex}\mathrm{IB}=6 \mathrm{A}^{-1}, \text { i.e., } \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}   
The given system of equations can be written as
AX = C, where
{tex}\mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right], \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ 17 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
The solution of the given system AX = C is given by X = A-1C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 2 & -4 \\ 2 & -1 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ 17 \\ 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array}{c} 6+34-28 \\ -12+34-28 \\ 6-17+34 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ -1 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence x = 2, y = 1 and z = 4

question_text: On the set R- {-1}, a binary operation is defined by a * b = a + b + ab for all a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R – {-1} Prove that * is commutative on R – {-1}. Find the identity element and prove that every element of R-{-1}is invertible.
question_html:
On the set R- {-1}, a binary operation is defined by
a * b = a + b + ab for all a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R – {-1}
Prove that * is commutative on R – {-1}. Find the identity element and prove that every element of R-{-1}is invertible.
solution_html:
Commutative: For any a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R - {-1}, We have a * b = a + b + ab and b * a = b + a + ba. But {by commutative property of addition and multiplication on R – {–1}, we have:
a + b + ab = b + a + ba
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a * b = b * a
Hence * is commutative on R - {-1}
Identity Element: Let e be the identity element.
Then a * e = e * a for all a {tex}\in{/tex} R - {-1}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + e + ae = a and e + a + ea = a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e(1 + a) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = 0 [since a {tex}\neq{/tex} -1)
Thus, 0 is the identity element for * defined on R - {-1}
Inverse: Let a {tex}\in{/tex} R - {-1} and let b be the inverse of a. Then
a * b = e = b * a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a * b = 0 = b * a ({tex}\because{/tex} e = 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b + ab = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow b=\frac{-a}{a+1} \in \mathbf{R}(\text { since } a \neq-1){/tex}
Moreover, {tex}\frac{-a}{a+1} \neq-1{/tex}. Thus {tex}b=\frac{-a}{a+1} \in \mathbf{R}-\{-1\}{/tex}
Hence, every element of R - {-1} is invertible and the inverse of an element a is {tex}\frac{-a}{a+1}{/tex}.

question_text: Prove that the perimeter of a right angled triangle of given hypotenuse is maximum when the triangle is isosceles.
question_html: Prove that the perimeter of a right angled triangle of given hypotenuse is maximum when the triangle is isosceles.
solution_text: Let H be the hypotenuse AC and {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between the hypotenuse and the base BC of the right angled triangle ABC. Then BC = base = H cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and AC = Perpendicular {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = Perimeter of right-angled triangle = H + H cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + Hsin {tex}\theta{/tex} = P For maximum or minimum of perimeter, {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{P}}{d \theta}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{H}(0-\sin \theta+\cos \theta)=0, \text { i.e. } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Now {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} \mathrm{P}}{d \theta^{2}}\right)=-\mathrm{H} \cos \theta-\mathrm{H} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} \mathrm{P}}{d \theta^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}=-\mathrm{H}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{H}<0{/tex} Thus P is maximum at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} For {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , Base {tex}=H \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{H}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and perpendicular {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{H}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence, the perimeter of a right-angled triangle is maximum when the triangle is isosceles.
solution_html:
Let H be the hypotenuse AC and {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between the hypotenuse and the base BC of the right angled triangle ABC.

Then BC = base = H cos{tex}\theta{/tex} and AC = Perpendicular
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = Perimeter of right-angled triangle
= H + H cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + Hsin{tex}\theta{/tex} = P
For maximum or minimum of perimeter, {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{P}}{d \theta}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{H}(0-\sin \theta+\cos \theta)=0, \text { i.e. } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Now
{tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} \mathrm{P}}{d \theta^{2}}\right)=-\mathrm{H} \cos \theta-\mathrm{H} \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} \mathrm{P}}{d \theta^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}=-\mathrm{H}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{H}<0{/tex}
Thus P is maximum at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
For {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, Base {tex}=H \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{H}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and perpendicular {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{H}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Hence, the perimeter of a right-angled triangle is maximum when the triangle is isosceles.

question_html: Using the method of integration, find the area of the region bounded by the lines 2x + y = 4, 3x - 2y = 6 and 3y + 5 = 0.
solution_text: x - 3y + 50 x = -5, x = 0 y = 0, {tex}y=\frac{5}{3}=16{/tex} Finding the point of intersection of given lines as A(1,2), B(4,3) and C (2,0) Therefore, required area {tex}=\int \limits_{1}^{4}\left(\frac{x+5}{3}\right) d x-\int \limits_{1}^{2}(4-2 x) d x-\int \limits_{2}^{4}\left(\frac{3 x-6}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left.\left.\left.=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+5 x\right)\right]^{4}-\left(4 x-x^{2}\right)\right]_{1}^{2}-\left(\frac{3}{4} x^{2}-3 x\right)\right]_{2}^{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{45}{2}-1-3=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{sq.} \text { units }{/tex}
solution_html:
x - 3y + 50
x = -5, x = 0
y = 0, {tex}y=\frac{5}{3}=16{/tex}
Finding the point of intersection of given lines as A(1,2), B(4,3) and C (2,0)
Therefore, required area
{tex}=\int \limits_{1}^{4}\left(\frac{x+5}{3}\right) d x-\int \limits_{1}^{2}(4-2 x) d x-\int \limits_{2}^{4}\left(\frac{3 x-6}{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\left.\left.\left.=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+5 x\right)\right]^{4}-\left(4 x-x^{2}\right)\right]_{1}^{2}-\left(\frac{3}{4} x^{2}-3 x\right)\right]_{2}^{4}{/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \limits_{1}^{4}\left(2 x^{2}-x\right) d x{/tex} as limit of a sum.
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \limits_{1}^{4}\left(2 x^{2}-x\right) d x{/tex} as limit of a sum.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int \limits_{1}^{4}\left(2 x^{2}-x\right) d x=\int \limits_{1}^{4} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}[f(1)+f(1+h)+f(1+2 h)+{/tex} ......+ f(1 + (n - 1) h)] ..... (i) Where {tex}h=\frac{4-1}{n}{/tex} , i.e., nh = 3 Now, {tex}f(1+\overline{n-1} h){/tex} = 2(1 + (n - 1)h) 2 - (1 + (n - 1)h) = 2(1 + (n - 1) 2 h 2 - 1(n + (n - 1)h) = 2(n - 1) 2 h 2 + 3(n - 1)h + 1 Therefore, f(1) = 2.0 2 h 2 + 3.0 h + 1, f(1 + h) = 2.1 2 h 2 + 3.1.h + 1 f(1 + 2h) = 2.2 2 h 2 + 3.2.h + 1, f(1 + (n - 1)h) = 2.2 2 h 2 + 3.2.h + 1 Thus, {tex}I=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h\left[n+2 \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6} h^{2}+\frac{3 n(n-1)(n h-h)}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h n+\frac{2(n h)(n h-h)(2 n h-h)}{6}+\frac{3(n h)(n h-h)}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left\{3+\frac{2(3)(3-h)(6-h)}{6}+\frac{3(3)(3-n)}{2}\right\}=\frac{69}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\int \limits_{1}^{4}\left(2 x^{2}-x\right) d x=\int \limits_{1}^{4} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}[f(1)+f(1+h)+f(1+2 h)+{/tex} ......+ f(1 + (n - 1) h)] ..... (i)
Where {tex}h=\frac{4-1}{n}{/tex}, i.e., nh = 3
Now, {tex}f(1+\overline{n-1} h){/tex} = 2(1 + (n - 1)h)2 - (1 + (n - 1)h)
= 2(1 + (n - 1)2h2 - 1(n + (n - 1)h) = 2(n - 1)2h2 + 3(n - 1)h + 1
Therefore, f(1) = 2.02h2 + 3.0 h + 1, f(1 + h) = 2.12h2 + 3.1.h + 1
f(1 + 2h) = 2.22h2 + 3.2.h + 1, f(1 + (n - 1)h) = 2.22h2 + 3.2.h + 1
Thus, {tex}I=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h\left[n+2 \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6} h^{2}+\frac{3 n(n-1)(n h-h)}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h n+\frac{2(n h)(n h-h)(2 n h-h)}{6}+\frac{3(n h)(n h-h)}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left\{3+\frac{2(3)(3-h)(6-h)}{6}+\frac{3(3)(3-n)}{2}\right\}=\frac{69}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular from the point (2, 3, 7) to the plane 3x – y – z = 7. Also, find the length of the perpendicular.
question_html: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular from the point (2, 3, 7) to the plane 3x – y – z = 7. Also, find the length of the perpendicular.
solution_text: The equation of line AB perpendicular to the given plane is {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{-1}=\frac{z-7}{-1}=\lambda(\text { say }){/tex} Therefore coordinates of the foot $\mathrm{B}$ of perpendicular drawn from A on the plane 3x - y - z = 7 will be {tex}(3 \lambda+2,-\lambda+3,-\lambda+7){/tex} Since {tex}B=(3 \lambda+2,-\lambda+3,-\lambda+7){/tex} lies on 3x - y - z = 7, we have {tex}=3(3 \lambda+2)-(-\lambda+3)-(-\lambda+7)=7 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} Hence the co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular is (5, 2, 6) and the length of perpendicular {tex}=\sqrt{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
The equation of line AB perpendicular to the given plane is
{tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{-1}=\frac{z-7}{-1}=\lambda(\text { say }){/tex}
Therefore coordinates of the foot $\mathrm{B}$ of perpendicular drawn from A on the plane 3x - y - z = 7 will be
{tex}(3 \lambda+2,-\lambda+3,-\lambda+7){/tex}
Since {tex}B=(3 \lambda+2,-\lambda+3,-\lambda+7){/tex} lies on 3x - y - z = 7, we have
{tex}=3(3 \lambda+2)-(-\lambda+3)-(-\lambda+7)=7 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}
Hence the co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular is (5, 2, 6) and the length of perpendicular {tex}=\sqrt{11}{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of the plane containing the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\mu(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} . Also, find the distance of this plane from the point (1, 1, 1)
question_html: Find the equation of the plane containing the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\mu(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex}. Also, find the distance of this plane from the point (1, 1, 1)
solution_text: The given lines are {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} ....(i) and {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\mu(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(ii) Note that line (i) passes through the point (1, 1, 0) and has d.r's, 1, 2, -1 , and line (ii) passes through the point (1, 1, 0) and has d.r.'s, -1, 1, -2 Since the required plane contain the lines (i) and (ii), the plane is parallel to the vectors {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} Therefore required plane is perpendicular to the vector {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{c}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Hence equation of required plane is {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad[\vec{r}-(\hat{i}+\hat{j})] \cdot(-3 \vec{i}+3 \vec{j}+3 \vec{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{r} \cdot(\vec{i}+\vec{j}+\vec{k})=0{/tex} and its cartesian form is -x + y + z = 0 Distance from (1, 1, 1) to the plane is {tex}\frac{|1(-1)+1.1+1.1|}{\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \text { unit }{/tex}
solution_html:
The given lines are
{tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} ....(i)
and {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\mu(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(ii)
Note that line (i) passes through the point (1, 1, 0)
and has d.r's, 1, 2, -1 , and line (ii) passes through the point (1, 1, 0) and has d.r.'s, -1, 1, -2
Since the required plane contain the lines (i) and (ii), the plane is parallel to the vectors
{tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Therefore required plane is perpendicular to the vector {tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{c}{/tex} and
{tex}\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
Hence equation of required plane is
{tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad[\vec{r}-(\hat{i}+\hat{j})] \cdot(-3 \vec{i}+3 \vec{j}+3 \vec{k})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{r} \cdot(\vec{i}+\vec{j}+\vec{k})=0{/tex}
and its cartesian form is -x + y + z = 0
Distance from (1, 1, 1) to the plane is
{tex}\frac{|1(-1)+1.1+1.1|}{\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \text { unit }{/tex}

question_text: Two cards are drawn successively without replacement from well-shuffled pack of 52 cards. Find the probability distribution of the number of kings. Also, calculate the mean and variance of the distribution.
question_html: Two cards are drawn successively without replacement from well-shuffled pack of 52 cards. Find the probability distribution of the number of kings. Also, calculate the mean and variance of the distribution.
solution_text: x 0 1 2 P(x) {tex}\frac{188}{221}{/tex} {tex}\frac{32}{221}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{221}{/tex} Now mean of {tex}x=\mathrm{E}(x)=\sum \limits_{i=1}^{n} x_{i} \mathrm{P}\left(x_{i}\right){/tex} {tex}=0 \times \frac{188}{221}+1 \times \frac{32}{221}+\frac{2 \times 1}{221}=\frac{34}{221}{/tex} Also {tex}E\left(x^{2}\right)=\sum \limits_{i=1}^{n} x i^{2} p(x i){/tex} {tex}=0^{2} \times \frac{188}{221}+1^{2} \times \frac{32}{221}+2^{2} \times \frac{1}{221}=\frac{36}{221}{/tex} Now var (x) = {tex}\mathrm{E}\left(x^{2}\right)-\left[\mathrm{E}(x)^{2}\right]=\frac{36}{221}-\left(\frac{34}{221}\right)^{2}=\frac{6800}{(221)^{2}}{/tex} Therefore standard deviation {tex}\sqrt{\operatorname{var}(x)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{6800}}{221}=0.37{/tex}

x 0 1 2
P(x) {tex}\frac{188}{221}{/tex} {tex}\frac{32}{221}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{221}{/tex}

Now mean of {tex}x=\mathrm{E}(x)=\sum \limits_{i=1}^{n} x_{i} \mathrm{P}\left(x_{i}\right){/tex}
{tex}=0 \times \frac{188}{221}+1 \times \frac{32}{221}+\frac{2 \times 1}{221}=\frac{34}{221}{/tex}
Also {tex}E\left(x^{2}\right)=\sum \limits_{i=1}^{n} x i^{2} p(x i){/tex}
{tex}=0^{2} \times \frac{188}{221}+1^{2} \times \frac{32}{221}+2^{2} \times \frac{1}{221}=\frac{36}{221}{/tex}

Therefore standard deviation {tex}\sqrt{\operatorname{var}(x)}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A dietician wishes to mix two types of foods in such a way that vitamin contents of the mixture contain at least 8 units of Vitamin A and 10 units of Vitamin C. Food ‘I’ contains 2 units/kg of Vitamin A and 1 unit/kg of Vitamin C. Food ‘II’ contains 1 unit/kg of Vitamin A and 2 units/kg of Vitamin C. It costs ₹50 per kg to purchase Food ‘I’ and ₹70 per kg to purchase Food ‘II’. Formulate this problem as a linear programming problem to minimise the cost of such a mixture and solve it graphically.
question_html: A dietician wishes to mix two types of foods in such a way that vitamin contents of the mixture contain at least 8 units of Vitamin A and 10 units of Vitamin C. Food ‘I’ contains 2 units/kg of Vitamin A and 1 unit/kg of Vitamin C. Food ‘II’ contains 1 unit/kg of Vitamin A and 2 units/kg of Vitamin C. It costs ₹50 per kg to purchase Food ‘I’ and ₹70 per kg to purchase Food ‘II’. Formulate this problem as a linear programming problem to minimise the cost of such a mixture and solve it graphically.
solution_text: Let the mixture contains x kg of food I and y kg of food II. Thus we have to minimise Z = 50x + 70y Subject to {tex}2 x+y \geq 8{/tex} {tex}x+2 y \geq 10{/tex} {tex}x, y \geq 0{/tex} The feasible region determined by the above inequalities is an unbounded region. Vertices of feasible region are A(0, 8) B(2, 4) C(10, 0) Now value of Z at A(0, 8) {tex}=50 \times 0+70 \times 8=560{/tex} B(2, 4) = 380 C(10, 0) = 500 As the feasible region is unbounded therefore, we have to draw the graph of x + 70y < 380 i.e. 5x + 7y < 38 As the resulting open half plane has no common point with feasible region thus the minimum value of z = 380 at B (2, 4). Hence, the optimal mixing strategy for the dietician would be to mix 2 kg of food I and 4 kg of food II to get the minimum cost of the mixture i.e ₹380.

Let the mixture contains x kg of food I and y kg of food II.

{tex}x, y \geq 0{/tex}

The feasible region determined by the above inequalities is an unbounded region. Vertices of feasible region are
A(0, 8) B(2, 4) C(10, 0)
Now value of Z at A(0, 8) {tex}=50 \times 0+70 \times 8=560{/tex}
B(2, 4) = 380 C(10, 0) = 500
As the feasible region is unbounded therefore, we have to draw the graph of
x + 70y < 380 i.e. 5x + 7y < 38
As the resulting open half plane has no common point with feasible region thus the minimum value of z = 380 at B (2, 4). Hence, the optimal mixing strategy for the dietician would be to mix 2 kg of food I and 4 kg of food II to get the minimum cost of the mixture i.e ₹380.
